papergraph.title: Multi‐Layer Charge Regulation Engineering for Efficient Enhancement of Aramid Epoxy‐Based Composite at Withstand Voltage Performance
papergraph.citation: Guowei Xia et al. (2026) Multi‐Layer Charge Regulation Engineering for Efficient Enhancement of Aramid Epoxy‐Based Composite at Withstand Voltage Performance doi:10.1002/adfm.76312
papergraph.venue: Advanced Functional Materials
papergraph.abstract: In recent years, fiber‐reinforced epoxy resin‐based composites have become commonly used for insulating components in high‐voltage transmission and transformation equipment. The high withstand voltage performance of materials is crucial to ensuring the safe and stable operation of equipment and the power grid. Therefore, in this paper, a multi‐layer charge regulation engineering was designed on the aramid fiber's surface to regulate the distribution and transmission of charges, thereby achieving an efficient enhancement of the withstand voltage of the composites. This engineering utilizes the charge trapping characteristics of insulating materials and the charge transport properties of semiconductor materials. The effectiveness of this engineering was validated through the tests of flashover voltage and breakdown strength. After constructing the optimal multi‐layer charge regulation structure, the flashover voltage increased by 62.03%, and the breakdown strength improved by 65.22%. Combining charge dissipation and trap distribution, interface bond strength, molecular dynamics simulations, and phase‐field modeling results reveals the mechanism of the multi‐layer charge regulation engineering. This can be summarized as the charge trapping in the F‐Group layer, uniform charge distribution in the ZnO nanowire layer, and deep charge transport in the FePc layer, which together ensure the effective enhancement of the material's withstand voltage performance.

papergraph.title: Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer
papergraph.citation: Naveen Garg et al. (2025) Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer doi:10.1109/CISES66934.2025.11264962
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent and Secure Engineering Solutions (CISES)
papergraph.abstract: The direct transmission systems using the voltage of one of the largest values, HVDC, that allow transmitting electricity with high efficiency and safety over long distances may be considered one of the most important milestones in the history of electricity engineering and development of the electrical power systems. It has a few strengths such as; it has a lower power loss compared with standard alternating current (AC) systems, is more stable on the grid, and has power density. The fundamental units of the HVDC technology are transformer stations, lines and control systems. HVDC converters technology has made the major focus on voltage source converters (VSCs) and line-commutated converters (LCCs) in power conversion between AC and DC to achieve maximum efficiency of the system. Renewable energy: Here HVDC can adopt renewable energy sources like wind power and solar energy to ease transfer of power through modular and long-distance power transmission of remote generation sites to the centre of consumption. Technology HVDC is being developed in the direction of higher converter efficiency, reduced cost and decreased fault tolerance. Enhanced control algorithms and real-time monitoring software guarantee dynamic stability and easy interface with current grid infrastructures. With the information on the cleaner energy future, an even further progress of the HVDC systems that will be incorporated in the world energy transition on the way to more sustainable and resilient electricity power systems is proceeding.

papergraph.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
papergraph.citation: Tongbo He et al. (2025) Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals doi:10.1109/ICEEPS66790.2025.11239566
papergraph.venue: 2025 4th International Conference on Energy and Electrical Power Systems (ICEEPS)
papergraph.abstract: As core equipment for grid condition monitoring, power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, including material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies. This paper proposes an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control. Through retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules, we established a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system. The implementation of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model enables precise simulation of altitude gradient variations. Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s), while significantly reducing testing cycle duration. This research provides technical support for reliability evaluation of power transmission equipment in ultra-high altitude regions such as the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, demonstrating substantial engineering application value.

papergraph.title: Application of Integrated Optical Electric-Field Sensor on the Measurements of Transient Voltages in AC High-Voltage Power Grids
papergraph.citation: S. Xie et al. (2019) Application of Integrated Optical Electric-Field Sensor on the Measurements of Transient Voltages in AC High-Voltage Power Grids doi:10.3390/APP9091951
papergraph.venue: Applied Sciences
papergraph.abstract: Transient voltages in the power grid are the key for the fault analysis of a power grid, optimized insulation design, and the standardization of the high-voltage testing method. The traditional measuring equipment, based on electrical engineering, normally has a limited bandwidth and response speed, which are also featured by a huge size and heavy weight. In this paper, an integrated optical electric-field sensor based on the Pockels effect was developed and applied to measure the transient voltages on the high-voltage conductors in a non-contact measuring mode. The measuring system has a response speed faster than 6 ns and a wide bandwidth ranging from 5 Hz to 100 MHz. Moreover, the sensors have the dimensions of 18 mm by 18 mm by 48 mm and a light weight of dozens of grams. The measuring systems were employed to monitor the lightning transient voltages on a 220 kV overhead transmission line. The switching transient voltages were also measured by the measuring system during the commissioning of the 500 kV middle Tibet power grid. In 2017, 307 lightning transient voltages caused by induction stroke were recorded. The characteristics of these voltage waveforms are different from the standard lightning impulse voltage proposed by IEC standards. Three types of typical switching transient voltage in 500 kV AC power grid were measured, and the peak values of these overvoltages can reach 1.73 times rated voltage.

papergraph.title: Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering
papergraph.citation: Xiaolong Wang et al. (2025) Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering doi:10.1109/ICPICS66386.2025.11347183
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Power, Intelligent Computing and Systems (ICPICS)
papergraph.abstract: In order to improve the flexibility and safety of power supply of electrical engineering systems in scenarios such as high-voltage isolation, complex environments and mobile devices, this paper studies wireless power transmission technology and its application in smart substations, comprehensively and systematically sorts out three types of wireless power supply mechanisms: electromagnetic induction, magnetic resonance coupling and microwave transmission, builds a wireless power supply system framework with magnetic resonance coupling as the core, and deploys the system in 110 kV smart substation scenarios. Test results show that the system achieves an average transmission efficiency of 78.6 % at a coupling distance of 2.8 meters, controls the power fluctuation rate within plus or minus 2.6 %, and reduces the annual interruption frequency to 3 times. The comprehensive operation stability and safety far exceed the traditional wired method. Wireless power transmission shows good engineering practice feasibility and expansion potential, and is suitable for key parts in smart grids that have high requirements for flexibility, safety and efficiency of energy supply.

papergraph.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects
papergraph.citation: Yuliang Jiang et al. (2026) Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects doi:10.1109/ICICACS68679.2026.11578464
papergraph.venue: 2026 4th International Conference on Integrated Circuits and Communication Systems (ICICACS)
papergraph.abstract: The cost-effective power transmission is the bulk movement of electrical energy form generating stations to substations located near demand centers. The long distance using high voltage lines and it serves as the critical bridge in the electrical grid, ensuring that energy produced as efficient, remote location reaches homes and industries. The conductors, insulators, towers and transformers contribute major portion of the total investment in power transmission and transformation projects. Selection of suitable main installation materials configurations is crucial for minimizing project costs while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability. The existing optimization algorithms focus on overall project investment and faced the inefficient energy transmission problem, when the existing algorithm are unable to concentrate the multiple crucial aspects simultaneously and stuck into the local optima. To mitigate this problem, the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) is used proposed. The PSO is used to concentrate the multiple aspects simultaneously based on the iterative search spaces utilizing swarm collective experience recursively. The SCT is used to overcome the premature convergence problem which is occurred by the PSO, by forcing the algorithm to resume the exploration when the progress is stopped. The proposed PSO-SCT framework is achieved better results comparing to some models such as Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO), Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO). Thus, the proposed PSO-SCT based approach optimizes conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation selection to reduce main installation materials cost while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.

papergraph.title: Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems
papergraph.citation: Rab Nawaz et al. (2026) Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems doi:10.1038/s41598-026-49195-7
papergraph.venue: Scientific Reports
papergraph.abstract: Modern power systems incorporate ultra-high-voltage transmission lines with series compensation to enhance their reliability and stability. These configurations generate complex data patterns in electrical signals, which present challenges for accurate fault diagnosis. This complexity arises from various potential faults, such as high-impedance faults and inductive-capacitive interactions, compounded by data scarcity owing to privacy and cybersecurity constraints. This study employs a simulation-based analysis to facilitate fault detection, classification, and localization as unified Multi-Classification tasks. The methodology improves the time-frequency domain data through meta-feature engineering and utilizes consensus-based feature selection by combining gradient boosting, variance thresholding, and mutual information. Model learning is conducted using Bayesian optimization within a Stacking Ensemble, integrating Extra Trees, Light Gradient Boosting Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting with an optimized Random Forest meta-classifier. The evaluation considers the accuracy, computational time, inference speed, and memory usage under challenging conditions to address practical limitations. The results indicate that the Stacking Ensemble achieves the highest performance despite its greater computational demand. Extra Trees offers the fastest execution but shows decreased accuracy with increased classification complexity. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine and Extreme Gradient Boosting demonstrate optimized performance across tasks owing to enhanced data processing. These findings confirm the practical applicability of the proposed fault diagnosis framework to modern ultra-high-voltage power systems.

papergraph.title: Research on system optimization and strategic path of China’s energy security from the perspective of electrical engineering
papergraph.citation: D. Danylchenko et al. (2025) Research on system optimization and strategic path of China’s energy security from the perspective of electrical engineering doi:10.20998/eree.2025.1(10).333342
papergraph.venue: Bulletin of the National Technical University "KhPI". Series: Energy: Reliability and Energy Efficiency
papergraph.abstract: The article presents a comprehensive study of China's energy security from a technical and economic perspective, taking into account current challenges and strategic goals of the energy transition. The main focus is on the quantitative analysis of structural problems that threaten the stability of the energy system. In particular, based on 2023 data, it was found that the structure of energy consumption remains dependent on fossil fuels: the share of coal is 56.4 %, and oil import dependence is 72.6 %. In addition, the probability of overloading the power grid in extreme weather conditions was found to be 7.3 %, indicating the system's vulnerability to climate change. The study modeled the effectiveness of the implementation of Smart Grid technologies, which increased the efficiency of ultra-high voltage power transmission by 18 %. The study also recorded a 40 % increase in the total capacity of virtual power plants and an increase in the internal rate of return of energy storage systems based on lithium batteries to 8.7 %. From the perspective of long-term strategic planning, the proposed multi-energy flow model estimates the cost of transporting 500 km of green hydrogen at 35 yuan/kg, and also forms a standardized power compensation mechanism (0.08 yuan/kW). The empirical results confirm the feasibility of such measures: the rate of rejection of new transmission lines in the northwestern regions has been reduced to 3.2 %, and the introduction of 12 GW of energy storage systems in the Yangtze Delta has reduced peak load by 15%. It is also demonstrated that the cost of hydrogen production from offshore wind energy has reached 0.3 yuan/kWh, or 35 yuan/kg, taking into account market electricity tariffs. The article proposes strategic measures to ensure energy security, including investments in scientific and technological development in the amount of 0.8 % of gross domestic product, modernization of the power grid with funding of 4.2 trillion yuan over the next 10 years, and the creation of an adaptive political monitoring system. These results form the basis for a systematic electrical engineering approach to ensuring China's energy sustainability in the context of global transformations.

papergraph.title: Comparing a Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current and Unified Power Flow Control for Short Circuit Level Reduction in Iraqi 400 Kv Grid
papergraph.citation: A. N. Merzah et al. (2024) Comparing a Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current and Unified Power Flow Control for Short Circuit Level Reduction in Iraqi 400 Kv Grid doi:10.51244/ijrsi.2024.1107018
papergraph.venue: International journal of research and scientific innovation
papergraph.abstract: The demand for electricity has increased significantly in recent years; yet, the transmission and distribution networks are unable to meet unrestrained demands because of resource constraint. Losses in power lines exacerbate the unfavorable conditions for maximum power transfer. Recent decades have seen a rise in the use of FACTS and High Voltage Direct Current transmission in power systems due to its many advantages, which include reduced short circuit level (SCL), enhanced voltage profiles, reduced network power losses, and enhanced system reliability and safety. The paper presents a comparing study of the effectiveness of Unified Power Flow Control (UPFC) and Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current (MT-HVDC) transmission in reducing short circuit current and network power losses for the Iraqi Super High Voltage (ISHV) grid. Modeling of Unified Power Flow Control and Multi-Terminal High Voltage Direct Current transmission is carried out using the Power System Simulator for Engineering (PSS/E) version 32 Package Program. The integration of multi-terminal HVDC transmission to the system resulted in a considerable decrease in short circuit levels and network power losses for the majority of buses in the system, as well as a reduction in the loading on the transmission lines in the southern region of Iraqi Super High Voltage grid, As a result, the South’s network performance will improve, hence improving Iraq’s electrical power system. Furthermore, when compared to installing a UPFC, the results showed that multi-terminal HVDC transmission reduces short circuit levels more efficiently.

papergraph.title: Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale
papergraph.citation: Ping Xiong et al. (2025) Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale doi:10.1109/ICPST65050.2025.11088518
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
papergraph.abstract: The rapid integration of variable renewable energy into county-level power grids has introduced significant operational challenges, particularly transmission congestion and transformer overload caused by bi-directional flow. To overcome the restriction of conventional grid reinforcement solutions focused mainly on infrastructure expansion, the work develops an integrated generation-grid-load-storage coordination framework adapted to scenarios with high penetration of renewables. The solution achieves localized energy balance through optimized energy storage allocation and operational modes control, establishing a resilient power grid architecture capable of grid-connected interaction and autonomous islanded operation. Renovation schemes of building a power system with ultra-high renewable penetration ratios are presented, in which technical routes for the coordination framework are investigated. Performance of key technologies, including grid-forming voltage source converters, smooth mode transition, and the collective control of the coordination system in support of the independent operation and stability of a 100% variable inverter-based renewable energy sources power system, is validated.

papergraph.title: Ultra-High Voltage NV Center Magnetic Sensing System Based on Power over Fiber
papergraph.citation: Linfeng Zhan et al. (2025) Ultra-High Voltage NV Center Magnetic Sensing System Based on Power over Fiber doi:10.3390/photonics12111093
papergraph.venue: Photonics
papergraph.abstract: Aiming to address the insulation and power supply challenges faced by electrical measurement in ultra-high voltage (UHV) environments, this study proposes and implements a nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center magnetic sensing system based on Power over Fiber (PoF) technology. The system adopts a high-voltage and low-voltage separation design, realizing the isolated transmission of electrical energy and the reliable recovery of measurement signals through an optical fiber link. The sensing unit on the high-voltage side is composed of NV center sensors, microwave excitation modules, and signal processing modules. Its power supply is provided by an independently developed high-power laser power converter (LPC) assembly via 830 nm optical fiber laser transmission. Under an optical input of 10 W, this assembly can achieve an electrical output of 4.88 W with a conversion efficiency of 48.9%. The experimental results show that the system can operate stably in a simulated UHV environment; by optimizing modulation parameters, the optimal magnetic measurement sensitivity reaches 6.1 nT/Hz1/2. This research provides a safe and reliable solution for the power supply and precise sensing of high-potential side equipment in UHV scenarios, and demonstrates the application potential of PoF technology in advanced sensing for power systems.

papergraph.title: NV centers magnetic sensing system in ultra-high-voltage utilizing power over fiber
papergraph.citation: Xiaofei Li et al. (2026) NV centers magnetic sensing system in ultra-high-voltage utilizing power over fiber doi:10.1088/1742-6596/3174/1/012058
papergraph.venue: Journal of Physics, Conference Series
papergraph.abstract: This paper proposes and experimentally studies a magnetic sensing system that employs nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond and utilizes power over fiber (PoF) technology for ultra-high voltage (UHV) scenarios. The structure and working principle of the system are introduced. The designed PoF link delivers a maximum power output of 3.8 W with an electrical transmission efficiency of 38%, while the designed sensor exhibits outstanding electromagnetic stability within the 2.867 GHz frequency band. Finally, the system ultimately attains a sensitivity of 6.1 nT/Hz1/2 for magnetic measurements.

papergraph.title: Implications of GB/T 22384 Revision for Standardized Testing of Power Grid Security and Stability Control Systems
papergraph.citation: Lei Fu et al. (2025) Implications of GB/T 22384 Revision for Standardized Testing of Power Grid Security and Stability Control Systems doi:10.1109/ICPST65050.2025.11089103
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
papergraph.abstract: The revision of China’s national standard GB/T 22384 addresses critical challenges posed by new-type power grids, characterized by large-scale renewable energy integration, ultra-high voltage AC/DC transmission, and cyber-physical system complexities. Aligned with China’s "dual carbon" strategy and the technical demands of New Power Systems, this revision introduces expanded testing scopes, enhanced network security protection requirements, and refined dynamic simulation methodologies. A case study of the Qing-Yu HVDC project demonstrates the effectiveness of closed-loop test based on real-time digital simulation (RTDS) in validating hierarchical security and stability control systems (SSCS), revealing latent defects such as redundant tripping commands during cascading faults. Innovations lie in integrating RTDS for closed-loop validation of multi-scenario strategies, introducing dynamic state synchronization to mitigate coordination failures, and optimizing lifecycle testing management. The revised standard establishes a robust framework for standardized testing, ensuring reliability and adaptability of SSCS in high-renewable, power electronics-dominated grids. From the main aspects of this revision and the results of engineering empirical tests, the testing methods and technical requirements for SSCS should evolve iteratively in sync with the development process and technological advancements of power systems. SSCS plays a crucial role in ensuring the secure and stable operation of power systems, and the standardization work cannot be achieved just in one effort.

papergraph.title: Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan
papergraph.citation: Jiming Song et al. (2023) Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan doi:10.1051/e3sconf/202337002001
papergraph.venue: E3S Web of Conferences
papergraph.abstract: Ultra-high voltage (UHV) power grid is a new transmission and transformation technology, which has the advantages of large transmission capacity, long transmission distance, low line loss, saving land resources and obvious economic benefits. Taking the Zhangbei-xiongan 1000kv AC high voltage power transmission and transformation project as an example, this paper constructs the eco-environmental index system of the UHV transmission and transformation project by using the pressure-state-response model, and constructs a set of 18 indexes eco-environmental index system, it mainly includes three aspects: the eco-environmental pressure index caused by the construction behavior in different construction stages of power transmission and transformation projects, and the eco-environmental state index given by on-the-spot investigation and study of projects, based on the construction unit on the ecological environment impact management response measures given indicators. The index system provides a basis for comprehensive and systematic evaluation of the impact of power transmission and distribution projects on the ecological environment, and finally draws the corresponding project construction evaluation conclusions. In this paper, the eco-environmental impact assessment index system of power transmission and transformation engineering has certain universality, and it has certain reference and guiding significance for the ecological environment evaluation of power transmission and transformation engineering.

papergraph.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables
papergraph.citation: Zifan Zhang et al. (2025) A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables doi:10.1109/ICLCSE66889.2025.11389670
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Low Carbon and Smart Energy (ICLCSE)
papergraph.abstract: In the context of accelerating urbanisation and the transformation of energy structures, high voltage power cables have emerged as a pivotal transmission method within contemporary power systems. The operational reliability of these entities has been demonstrated to have a direct impact on grid security and power supply quality. Within cable structures, the metallic shielding layer fulfils a dual function: it both suppresses electromagnetic interference and protects communication facilities, while also providing a safe return path for ground faults and preventing electric shock accidents. The present study investigates the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in cable shields. Utilising the electromagnetic transient simulation software PSCAD, a refined cable model incorporating frequency-dependent parameters was constructed. The electrical characteristics and influencing factors of such faults were revealed through analysis of the variation patterns of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions. The present research provides both theoretical and empirical support for the rapid diagnosis and condition assessment in engineering applications.

papergraph.scenario: grounding faults in the metal shield layer of high-voltage power cables
papergraph.benchmark: shield currents and zero-sequence currents

papergraph.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines
papergraph.citation: Wei Lv et al. (2023) The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines doi:10.1088/1742-6596/2557/1/012065
papergraph.venue: Journal of Physics: Conference Series
papergraph.abstract: As an important part of the substation, the seismic capacity of ultra-high voltage (UHV) electrical equipment is related to the safe and stable operation of the substation and the whole power grid. The UHV gas-insulated transmission lines (GIL) have many structural characteristics, such as long pipeline distance, the large span between supports and the obvious amplification effect of supports. Its natural frequency is close to the predominant frequency of the seismic wave, resulting in its susceptibility to resonance or resonance-like and high seismic susceptibility. In order to analyze the seismic performance of UHV GIL better, the vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method and the dynamic time-history analysis method are used to analyze the seismic response of UHV GIL in this paper. The search for seismic weak positions of GIL is a reference for effectively improving the seismic performance of UHV GIL.
papergraph.method: response spectrum method and the dynamic time-history analysis
papergraph.scenario: seismic response of ultra-high voltage gas-insulated transmission lines
papergraph.benchmark: seismic weak positions
papergraph.contribution: analysis of the seismic response of UHV GIL and identification of seismic weak positions for improving seismic performance

papergraph.title: Unlocking the Hidden Capacity of the Electrical Grid Through Smart Transformer and Smart Transmission
papergraph.citation: M. Liserre et al. (2023) Unlocking the Hidden Capacity of the Electrical Grid Through Smart Transformer and Smart Transmission doi:10.1109/JPROC.2022.3157162
papergraph.venue: Proceedings of the IEEE
papergraph.abstract: Power systems are experiencing a rapid and dramatic transformation driven by the massive integration of non-dispatchable renewable energy sources such as wind and solar and highly variable loads such as electric vehicles and air conditioning. This challenges existing grid assets eventually leading to update them which in turn increase significantly the costs of the sustainable technologies. Power electronics is a pivotal technology for electrical power processing for renewable energies and sustainable transportation. By means of ”smart” functionalities power electronics converters already embedded in such applications, can also contribute to guaranteeing the overall system stable operation. Anyway this cooperative contribution from distributed devices may be not enough leading to the need in the voltage transformation and power transmission of ”system level” power electronics solutions. In the case of large charging stations a Smart Transformer while in the case of large solar and wind parks integration Medium or High Voltage Direct Current Transmission are system level solutions. This paper wants to review the potential of using such infrastructures to increase the capacity of existing grid assets avoiding or deferring their upgrade and hence reducing the overall costs of renewables integration and of the electrification of transport sector. In fact the power converters embedded in Smart Transformer (ST) and High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) can provide fast frequency and voltage response and precise control of power flow acting at system level much more effectively and feasibly for system operators as the distributed power converters embedded in several small sources and users. This paper reviews for the first time these two key power electronics ”system level” solutions together - Smart Transformer and High Voltage Direct Current - starting from their basic functionalities and showings how they can go beyond them showing how with grid forming functionalities they can offer new ”Smart Grid” tools to enhance the capability of the existing electric grid infrastructures.

I. I NTRODUCTION
Electrical grids are suffering large demand variability
caused mainly by highly variable loads such as air con-
ditioning and electric vehicles [1]. The broader integration
of renewable energies such as wind and solar is expected
to worsen the situation resulting in further congestion and
underutilization of the electrical network [2]. Most of these
loads and energy sources are connected to the grid by using
power electronics converters.
However during the last years, it has been registered not
only a steadily increasing number of distributed power elec-
tronics converters but also ”system level” power electronics
converters have been embedded into the electrical grid to
perform a multitude of tasks, as shown in Fig. 1. Due to
the fast dynamical response of currents and voltages, these
converters are used in a STA Tic COMpensator (STA TCOM)
to control reactive power, maintaining the voltage amplitude
at a given level and increasing the transmission capability of
the lines. Also, they are employed to manage Energy Storage
Systems (ESS) to inject active power providing fast frequency
regulation. High V oltage dc (HVDC) systems offer a cost-
effective solution for long-distance bulk energy transmission.
Smart Transformers (ST) provide voltage level transformation
and a wide variety of ancillary services such as reactive power
compensation, harmonic ﬁltering, voltage sag compensation,
frequency regulation, dc grids, among others [3].
Despite distributed and system-level power converters have
become an essential component of power grids [4], they are
usually associated with the reduction of system inertia, sub-
synchronous oscillations, and other problems. Recent analyses
show that a change in the control strategies can attenuate these
issues allowing to take real advantage of the controllability and
ﬂexibility power converters bring to the electrical grid [5].
Power electronics-interfaced renewable energies, such as
wind turbines and PV plants, offer no rotational inertia to
the system. This reduced inertia increases the rate of change
of frequency (RoCoF) that may trigger frequency relays and
causes the automatic under-frequency load shedding. Alterna-
tive frequency support methods for low inertia power systems,
such as virtual inertia, have been proposed. The virtual inertia

papergraph.title: Knowledge reasoning in power grid infrastructure projects based on deep multi-view graph convolutional network
papergraph.citation: Jie Hu et al. (2024) Knowledge reasoning in power grid infrastructure projects based on deep multi-view graph convolutional network doi:10.3389/fenrg.2023.1339416
papergraph.venue: Frontiers in Energy Research
papergraph.abstract: With the rapid development of power grid infrastructure, especially the increasing number of ultra-high voltage (UHV) projects, knowledge extracted from historical engineering data is collected and can be potentially used to assist in the review of power transmission and transformation projects. However, conventional knowledge modeling and knowledge reasoning methods cannot meet the current needs of power grid construction. In this paper, considering the more supernumerary and distinctive information brought by multi-view data which could be beneficial for feature representation and knowledge reasoning from the constructed knowledge base, a multi-view graph convolutional network (GCN) based on knowledge graph is proposed to make classification for power grid infrastructure projects. Specifically, several views are constructed based on attribute information of a knowledge graph. In addition, a Haar convolution-based pooling mechanism is employed to capture the structural features represented by a chain of subgraphs. And then an aggregator that combines both attribute and structural information is used to classify UHV projects. Results from both UHV and NCI-1 datasets indicate that our proposed method is more has higher accuracy and generalization ability.

papergraph.benchmark: accuracy and generalization ability

papergraph.title: Study on Reactive Power Response Characteristics of Grid-Forming Renewable Energy Systems with Virtual Internal Potential
papergraph.citation: Xiaofei Sun et al. (2024) Study on Reactive Power Response Characteristics of Grid-Forming Renewable Energy Systems with Virtual Internal Potential doi:10.1109/APPEEC61255.2024.10922351
papergraph.venue: IEEE PES Asia-Pacific Power and Energy Engineering Conference
papergraph.abstract: The wind energy resources and load distribution in China are inversely correlated. To meet the demand for long-distance power transmission from large-scale clean energy bases in desert areas, ultra-high voltage (UHV) transmission lines have become a strategic choice for the development of China's power grid. However, the lack of conventional synchronous power support in the AC sending-end system poses challenges when Commutation Failures or DC Blocking occur at the DC receiving-end, leading to transient overvoltage issues. These problems can result in large-scale disconnection of wind turbines, thereby limiting the ability of the sending-end grid to integrate renewable energy. Suppressing transient overvoltage in the sending-end grid imposes higher requirements on the reactive power support response speed of renewable energy sources. Therefore, this paper investigates the reactive power response characteristics of grid-forming renewable energy systems. It analyzes the influence of various factors such as different control coefficients, voltage measurement delays, and current loop control parameters on the reactive power response characteristics of grid-forming converters. This research provides theoretical support for the design of control strategies, parameter tuning, and capacity configuration of grid-forming renewable energy systems.

papergraph.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
papergraph.citation: Hyeongmeen Baik et al. (2025) Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications doi:10.1109/TPEL.2026.3658517
papergraph.venue: arXiv
papergraph.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) power supply topologies, aiming to bridge the gap between DBD applications and power electronics design. Two key aspects are examined: the dependence of the DBD electrical model on reactor geometry, and application-driven requirements for injected waveform characteristics, including shapes, voltage amplitude, frequency, and modulation techniques. On this basis, the paper systematically reviews two major categories of power supplies: sinusoidal types comprising transformerless and transformer-based resonant inverters, and pulsed power supplies (PPSs). The review summarizes performance trade-offs, highlights untested topologies and emerging applications, and offers guidance for advancing high-performance DBD power supply design for next-generation systems.

papergraph.title: Quantifying and mitigating electrical and environmental impacts of corona discharge
papergraph.citation: Ahmed M. Zobaa et al. (2025) Quantifying and mitigating electrical and environmental impacts of corona discharge doi:10.1038/s41598-025-26521-z
papergraph.venue: Scientific Reports
papergraph.abstract: Corona discharge has been recognized for centuries, with sailors reporting the bluish glow of St. Elmo’s fire on ship masts during storms. In the early development of high-voltage engineering, researchers such as Townsend and Peek described the physical basis of this phenomenon as the ionization of air around a conductor when the electric field exceeds the strength of the surrounding medium. The result is a partial discharge that produces visible light, hissing sounds, ozone, and other reactive gases, while also creating radio interference and ultraviolet radiation. In modern transmission systems, these effects appear as wasted power, accelerated wear of insulators, shortened equipment lifetime, and environmental concerns. Although corona has been studied for decades, it continues to challenge the reliable and economical operation of high-voltage networks, particularly under changing weather conditions. This study investigates the phenomenon by analyzing its causes, effects, and mitigation strategies through a combination of theoretical modelling, simulation, and statistical analysis. Using MATLAB Simulink and Python, simulations were conducted under varying environmental conditions—including temperature, humidity, and pressure—as well as electrical parameters such as voltage and conductor design, using observed data to ensure practical relevance. Comparable data sources may be used in other national or regional contexts. Key statistical techniques, including linear and multiple regression, analysis of variance (ANOVA), t-tests, and Monte Carlo simulations, were applied to determine the most influential factors affecting corona discharge losses. Results confirmed that higher voltage levels and unfavorable environmental conditions significantly increase corona loss, while increased conductor spacing and the use of corona rings emerged as the most effective mitigation strategies. An economic analysis based on probabilistic modelling estimated potential annual savings of up to 455 million Egyptian pounds (EGP) for the Egyptian grid, serving as a representative case study. The analytical framework is general and can be applied to other national transmission systems with appropriate data. The findings offer data-driven insights for improving transmission efficiency, minimizing power losses, and enhancing the overall reliability and cost-effectiveness of high-voltage power systems.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The developed index system can be used as a template for evaluating the ecological and environmental impacts of other UHV AC transmission and transformation projects, offering a structured and comprehensive approach for future assessments.

keynotes.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables

keynote.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables
keynote.core_problem: Understanding and analyzing the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in the metallic shielding layer of high-voltage power cables to improve grid security and power supply quality.
contributions: Investigation into the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in cable shields.
Development of a refined cable model incorporating frequency-dependent parameters using PSCAD software.
Analysis of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions.
methods: Electromagnetic transient simulation using PSCAD software.
Construction of a refined cable model with frequency-dependent parameters.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulation results showing the variation patterns of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions.

gap_signals.text: The present research provides both theoretical and empirical support for the rapid diagnosis and condition assessment in engineering applications.

code_or_implementation: PSCAD software for electromagnetic transient simulation.

important_claims.claim: High voltage power cables are pivotal in contemporary power systems, and their reliability impacts grid security and power supply quality.
important_claims.evidence: In the context of accelerating urbanisation and the transformation of energy structures, high voltage power cables have emerged as a pivotal transmission method within contemporary power systems. The operational reliability of these entities has been demonstrated to have a direct impact on grid security and power supply quality.

important_claims.claim: The metallic shielding layer in cables serves multiple critical functions, including suppressing electromagnetic interference and providing a safe return path for ground faults.
important_claims.evidence: Within cable structures, the metallic shielding layer fulfils a dual function: it both suppresses electromagnetic interference and protects communication facilities, while also providing a safe return path for ground faults and preventing electric shock accidents.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The refined cable model and simulation results can be used by researchers and engineers to better understand and diagnose grounding faults in high-voltage power cables, leading to improved design and maintenance practices.

keynotes.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines

keynote.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines
keynote.core_problem: Analyzing the seismic performance and identifying weak positions in ultra-high voltage (UHV) gas-insulated transmission lines (GIL) to improve their seismic capacity, which is crucial for the safe and stable operation of substations and the power grid.
contributions: Use of vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method and dynamic time-history analysis method to analyze the seismic response of UHV GIL.
methods: Vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method
Dynamic time-history analysis method
assumptions: The natural frequency of UHV GIL is close to the predominant frequency of the seismic wave, making it susceptible to resonance or resonance-like effects and high seismic susceptibility.

gap_signals.text: There is a need for more advanced or additional methods to fully understand and enhance the seismic performance of UHV GIL.

important_claims.claim: UHV GIL has structural characteristics that make it particularly vulnerable to seismic events, such as long pipeline distances, large spans between supports, and significant support amplification effects.
important_claims.evidence: The paper identifies these structural features and explains why they contribute to the high seismic susceptibility of UHV GIL.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a foundation for further research into the seismic resilience of UHV GIL, offering specific methodologies that can be applied or adapted in future studies to enhance the seismic design and safety of these critical components in the power grid.

keynotes.title: Unlocking the Hidden Capacity of the Electrical Grid Through Smart Transformer and Smart Transmission

keynote.title: Unlocking the Hidden Capacity of the Electrical Grid Through Smart Transformer and Smart Transmission
keynote.core_problem: The integration of non-dispatchable renewable energy sources and highly variable loads, such as electric vehicles and air conditioning, challenges the existing grid infrastructure, leading to a need for costly upgrades.
contributions: Reviewing the potential of using Smart Transformers (ST) and High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) to increase the capacity of existing grid assets without or with deferred upgrades, thereby reducing overall costs associated with renewable energy integration and electrification of the transport sector.
assumptions: Power electronics converters embedded in Smart Transformers and HVDC can provide fast frequency and voltage response and precise control of power flow, acting more effectively at a system level than distributed power converters in small sources and users.

gap_signals.text: Distributed devices' cooperative contribution may not be sufficient, necessitating system-level power electronics solutions like Smart Transformers and HVDC.

important_claims.claim: Smart Transformers and HVDC can significantly enhance the capability of existing electrical grid infrastructures by providing new 'Smart Grid' tools.
important_claims.evidence: These technologies offer fast frequency and voltage response and precise control of power flow, which are crucial for stable operation of the grid with high penetration of renewables and variable loads.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a comprehensive review of how Smart Transformers and HVDC can be leveraged to improve the capacity and efficiency of the electrical grid, offering a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners working on grid modernization and renewable energy integration.

keynotes.title: Knowledge reasoning in power grid infrastructure projects based on deep multi-view graph convolutional network

keynote.title: Knowledge reasoning in power grid infrastructure projects based on deep multi-view graph convolutional network
keynote.core_problem: Conventional knowledge modeling and reasoning methods are insufficient for the current needs of power grid construction, especially with the increasing number of ultra-high voltage (UHV) projects.
contributions: Proposed a multi-view graph convolutional network (GCN) based on a knowledge graph for classifying power grid infrastructure projects.
methods: Constructed several views based on attribute information of a knowledge graph.
Employed a Haar convolution-based pooling mechanism to capture structural features represented by a chain of subgraphs.
Used an aggregator that combines both attribute and structural information to classify UHV projects.
experiments_or_evidence: Results from both UHV and NCI-1 datasets indicate higher accuracy and generalization ability of the proposed method.

gap_signals.text: The conventional methods for knowledge modeling and reasoning are not sufficient, indicating a need for more advanced techniques.

important_claims.claim: The proposed multi-view GCN has higher accuracy and generalization ability compared to conventional methods.
important_claims.evidence: Results from both UHV and NCI-1 datasets support this claim.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The multi-view GCN approach can be adapted or extended to other domains requiring complex feature representation and classification, particularly where multi-source data is available.

keynotes.title: Study on Reactive Power Response Characteristics of Grid-Forming Renewable Energy Systems with Virtual Internal Potential

keynote.title: Study on Reactive Power Response Characteristics of Grid-Forming Renewable Energy Systems with Virtual Internal Potential
keynote.core_problem: The lack of conventional synchronous power support in the AC sending-end system poses challenges when Commutation Failures or DC Blocking occur at the DC receiving-end, leading to transient overvoltage issues and large-scale disconnection of wind turbines, thereby limiting the ability of the sending-end grid to integrate renewable energy.
contributions: Investigates the reactive power response characteristics of grid-forming renewable energy systems

Analyzes the influence of various factors such as different control coefficients, voltage measurement delays, and current loop control parameters on the reactive power response characteristics of grid-forming converters

gap_signals.text: This research provides theoretical support for the design of control strategies, parameter tuning, and capacity configuration of grid-forming renewable energy systems, suggesting that more practical and detailed studies are needed.

important_claims.claim: Suppressing transient overvoltage in the sending-end grid imposes higher requirements on the reactive power support response speed of renewable energy sources.
important_claims.evidence: The paper investigates the reactive power response characteristics to address the transient overvoltage issues caused by the lack of conventional synchronous power support in the AC sending-end system.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings can be used to inform the design and optimization of control strategies, parameter tuning, and capacity configuration for grid-forming renewable energy systems, particularly in scenarios where there is a need for rapid reactive power support.

keynotes.title: 500kV Ultra-High Voltage Cable Fine Manufacturing Technology Research

keynote.title: 500kV Ultra-High Voltage Cable Fine Manufacturing Technology Research
keynote.core_problem: Developing and refining the manufacturing technology for 500kV ultra-high voltage cables to meet the demands of long-distance, high-capacity, and high-voltage power transmission in urban settings.
contributions: Refinement of key core processes such as conductor stranding, insulation extrusion, wrinkled aluminum sleeve, and hot melt adhesive spraying.
Utilization of digitalization, intelligence, and informatization in the management of the manufacturing process.
Successful completion of the 500kV ultra-high voltage cable project, demonstrating the capability for localized production of high-end cables.
methods: Conductor stranding
Insulation extrusion
Wrinkled aluminum sleeve application
Hot melt adhesive spraying

gap_signals.text: The project is the first 500kV ultra-high voltage urban power grid (cable) project of China Southern Power Grid, indicating a significant step forward in the field.

important_claims.claim: Qingdao Hanhe Cable Co., Ltd. successfully completed the project product manufacturing, reflecting the ability for localized 500kV ultra-high voltage cable production.
important_claims.evidence: The company utilized years of experience in ultra-high voltage cable manufacturing and refined key processes, along with modern management methods, to complete the project.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides valuable insights into the refinement of manufacturing processes and the use of modern management techniques for producing high-voltage cables, which can be applied to similar projects and further research in the field of ultra-high voltage cable manufacturing.

keynotes.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications

keynote.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
keynote.core_problem: Bridging the gap between dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) applications and power electronics design, focusing on the electrical model's dependence on reactor geometry and the requirements for waveform characteristics.
contributions: Comprehensive review of DBD power supply topologies
Examination of the influence of reactor geometry on the DBD electrical model
Analysis of application-driven requirements for waveform characteristics
Systematic review of sinusoidal and pulsed power supplies, including their performance trade-offs
Highlighting untested topologies and emerging applications in DBD power supplies

gap_signals.text: Mentions emerging applications, indicating new fields or uses that could benefit from advanced DBD power supplies.
The paper highlights untested topologies, suggesting areas where further research is needed.

important_claims.claim: The review provides a systematic overview of DBD power supply topologies.
important_claims.evidence: The paper covers both sinusoidal types and pulsed power supplies, summarizing their performance trade-offs.

important_claims.claim: Guidance for high-performance DBD power supply design is offered.
important_claims.evidence: The paper aims to advance the design of next-generation systems by reviewing current and potential future technologies.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The structured review and highlighted gaps can guide researchers in developing new DBD power supply designs and exploring untested topologies and emerging applications, which are crucial for advancing the field.

keynotes.title: Quantifying and mitigating electrical and environmental impacts of corona discharge

keynote.title: Quantifying and mitigating electrical and environmental impacts of corona discharge
keynote.core_problem: Corona discharge in high-voltage transmission systems leads to wasted power, accelerated wear of insulators, shortened equipment lifetime, and environmental concerns, particularly under changing weather conditions.
contributions: Investigation of the causes, effects, and mitigation strategies of corona discharge through theoretical modeling, simulation, and statistical analysis.
Development of an analytical framework that can be applied to other national transmission systems with appropriate data.
Estimation of potential annual savings of up to 455 million Egyptian pounds (EGP) for the Egyptian grid as a representative case study.
methods: Theoretical modeling
Simulation using MATLAB Simulink and Python
Statistical analysis including linear and multiple regression, ANOVA, t-tests, and Monte Carlo simulations
experiments_or_evidence: Simulations conducted under varying environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, pressure) and electrical parameters (voltage, conductor design).
Economic analysis based on probabilistic modeling.
assumptions: The observed data used in simulations is representative and practical for the study.
limitations: The study's findings are based on the specific context of the Egyptian grid and may require adjustments for other national or regional contexts.

gap_signals.text: The study's findings are based on the specific context of the Egyptian grid and may require adjustments for other national or regional contexts.
The analytical framework can be applied to other national transmission systems with appropriate data, suggesting the need for further research in different contexts.

datasets_or_materials: Observed data on environmental and electrical parameters for simulations.
code_or_implementation: MATLAB Simulink and Python code for simulations.

important_claims.claim: Higher voltage levels and unfavorable environmental conditions significantly increase corona loss.
important_claims.evidence: Results from simulations and statistical analysis.

important_claims.claim: Increased conductor spacing and the use of corona rings are the most effective mitigation strategies.
important_claims.evidence: Results from simulations and statistical analysis.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The analytical framework and methodologies can be adapted and applied to other national or regional high-voltage transmission systems to improve transmission efficiency, minimize power losses, and enhance overall reliability and cost-effectiveness.
